actions.label: Epic Life Membership
actions.description: A done-for-you wealth concierge — a team that runs your financial playbook every month (protection, credit, banking, your tax-free policy, then passive income). Because they do the work, enrolling never costs you energy or one of your monthly moves — their action runs on top of yours each turn. ⚠️ First game? Try a run without it.
actions.lesson: The wealthy don't run the whole financial playbook themselves — they hire specialists who run it for them. A concierge trades a monthly fee for time and flawless execution: the protective and wealth-building moves happen on schedule whether or not you remember them. (This is a real service — the game just lets you feel the value.)
actions.narrative_success: Welcome to Epic Life. Your concierge team is on it — from here the financial playbook runs in the background. Watch for the 🌟 Epic Life updates each month as each piece clicks into place.

actions.label: Hire a Fractional CFO
actions.description: Bring on a part-time CFO who flags your best finance move each month and tidies your credit so you can borrow more, cheaper.
actions.lesson: A fractional CFO is the cheapest way to start thinking like the wealthy: structure, credit, and tax working as a system. Part-time now — but even part-time, a good CFO lets you borrow more and safer than you could alone.
actions.narrative_success: Your fractional CFO is in. They highlight the smartest finance move each month and have already widened your borrowing capacity a little — the start of leveraging harder and safer.
actions.narrative_failure: The fractional CFO is still untangling your books. Guidance starts next month.

actions.label: Promote CFO to Full-Time
actions.description: Promote your CFO to full-time to unlock far more credit, cheaper debt, and safe higher leverage — turning OPM into your growth engine.
actions.lesson: This is the real unlock: a great CFO lets you leverage harder AND safer — more credit, better rates, higher safe utilization — so other people's money funds your growth instead of your own cash. That's how the wealthy scale fast. But you can only afford the CFO once that leverage is already working: the chicken-and-egg ceiling that separates operators from owners.
actions.narrative_success: Your full-time CFO is in the seat. Credit lines widen, your rates drop, and you can safely carry more leverage — OPM is now doing the heavy lifting on growth. The salary is elite; the capital efficiency more than pays for it.
actions.narrative_failure: The full-time CFO is restructuring the capital stack; the bigger credit and rate improvements land shortly. Payroll hits now.

actions.label: Open a Private Banking Relationship
actions.description: For $2M+ clients: deposit to earn ~5%/yr while borrowing against it at just 1%/yr. Your money keeps earning as near-free cash funds your deals.
actions.lesson: This is how the ultra-wealthy leverage hardest: never sell your assets, never stop earning on them — borrow against them at near-zero rates instead. Your deposit earns ~5% while you borrow at 1%, so the spread is free money and the borrowed cash compounds into more assets. OPM, perfected.
actions.narrative_failure: The private bank approved your relationship, but the deposit and credit facility are still being set up — your interest and 1% line go live next month.

actions.label: Invest in a Private Equity Fund
actions.description: Commit roughly half of your available cash to a PE fund scaling private companies. It locks up (illiquid), but it's marked up the moment you're in and pays monthly distributions — returns public markets can't match. The exact amount committed shows on the result.
actions.lesson: Private equity is how the wealthy earn outsized, mostly-passive returns — owning slices of real businesses, marked up over time, throwing off distributions. Illiquidity is the price of the premium: you trade access-anytime for higher compounding.
actions.narrative_failure: The capital call went through, but the fund's first deals are still closing — distributions start once they deploy.

actions.label: Set Up a Family Office
actions.description: The billionaire setup: a dedicated team running your investments, taxes, estate, and giving as one coordinated machine.
actions.lesson: A family office turns scattered wealth into a coordinated system — investment, tax, legal, and legacy under one roof. It costs real money to run, but at scale the tax savings and better decisions pay for it many times over.
actions.narrative_success: Your family office is running. A dedicated team now coordinates your investments, taxes, estate, and giving — and your portfolio's yield ticks up as they optimize allocation. Runs about $5,000/mo, and it earns its keep.
actions.narrative_failure: The family office is staffed but still onboarding your accounts. Coordination — and the efficiency gains — ramp up over the next month.

actions.label: Establish a Dynasty Trust
actions.description: Move assets into a multi-generational trust — shielded from lawsuits, estate taxes, and creditors, passing to heirs intact for generations.
actions.lesson: The ultra-wealthy don't just build wealth — they make it permanent. A dynasty trust removes assets from your taxable estate and walls them off from lawsuits, letting wealth compound across generations without being taxed at each death. Legacy, engineered.
actions.narrative_success: The dynasty trust is funded. Your wealth now sits outside your estate — protected from lawsuits and estate tax, compounding for generations. Your great-grandchildren will benefit from this signature.
actions.narrative_failure: The trust is drafted and partially funded — the attorney is still moving assets in. Protection is building.

actions.label: Convene a Board of Directors
actions.description: Install a board to govern the company. With your execs running it and a board overseeing, the business runs without you — put it on autopilot.
actions.lesson: The final stage of leverage is governance. When executives run operations and a board provides oversight, the founder becomes truly optional — free to live, invest, and lead at the highest level. This is what 'the business runs without you' actually looks like.
actions.narrative_success: Your board is seated. Between your executive team and board oversight, the company governs itself — use “Let the Board run the month” any time to autopilot, and spend your energy on the big moves only you can make.
actions.narrative_failure: The board is forming — a couple of seats still to fill. Governance is taking shape.

actions.label: Form LLC & Set Up Books
actions.description: Form your LLC, open a business bank account, and set up bookkeeping — making your business a real legal entity with clean, separate finances.
actions.lesson: Separate yourself from the business from day one. The LLC shields your personal assets, the business account stops commingling, and bookkeeping lets you prove income to lenders — the foundation everything else is built on.
actions.narrative_success: The LLC is filed, the EIN issued, a business checking account is open, and your books are live. Your business exists as its own legal person with clean financials — and bookkeeping runs about $150/mo from here.
actions.narrative_failure: The LLC is filed and the account is open, but the bookkeeping setup is half-done — categories are a work in progress. Still running about $150/mo.

actions.label: Build Your D&B Business Credit Profile
actions.description: Get a D-U-N-S number, business phone/address/website, and net-30 vendor accounts so lenders see business credit, not just your personal score.
actions.lesson: Lenders check your BUSINESS credit (D&B / Paydex), not just your personal score. A DUNS number, a real address and phone, a web/social presence, and vendor tradelines that report on-time payments build a business credit identity — so the company borrows on its own name, off your personal guarantee. Each business credit line you add deepens the file too.
actions.narrative_success: D-U-N-S number issued. Business phone, address, website and socials are live, and three net-30 vendors are reporting your on-time payments. Your D&B profile is officially building — and climbs as the tradelines age and you add credit history.
actions.narrative_failure: Paperwork delays on the DUNS registration held things up — resubmit next month.

actions.label: Build Personal Credit
actions.description: Dispute errors and clear collections to rebuild your score over a few months — and your card issuer may automatically raise your limit, which lowers utilization without you paying anything down.
actions.lesson: Building credit is mechanics, not magic. The biggest levers are on-time payments and low utilization — keep card balances well under 30% of the limit. Disputing genuine errors and resolving collections helps too. Start early if you're carrying collections; the cleanup takes a couple of months to show.
actions.narrative_success: You pulled all three reports, challenged the genuine errors, knocked down a couple of balances, and put a plan against the collections. The corrections take 30-90 days, and you'll watch your score climb month over month as the file cleans up.
actions.narrative_failure: Mixed-up paperwork stalled the disputes and one collection wouldn't budge — only a little movement this month. Run it again to keep the cleanup going.

actions.label: Aggressive Debt Paydown
actions.description: Use spare cash to pay down revolving balances toward the 30% utilization sweet spot — the fastest lever on your credit score.
actions.lesson: Utilization (how much of your revolving limit you're using) is one of the biggest levers on your credit score — get it under 30%. Don't rush to kill cheap installment loans; that money is better kept working unless your debt-to-income is also too high.
actions.narrative_success: Two thousand dollars gone from the balance. The interest savings alone will compound from here.
actions.narrative_failure: Paid down most of it. An unexpected expense ate part of the payment. Still progress.

actions.label: Restructure & Optimize Your Debt
actions.description: The full debt playbook in one move: drop utilization, shift balances onto business credit (off your personal report), and pull working cash.
actions.lesson: The optimal debt move in one sequence: revolving → installment drops your utilization, then moving it onto business credit takes it off your personal report entirely, and the 0% intro buys time. Same debt, restructured into a higher score and usable cash.
actions.narrative_failure: The expert moved one balance and opened a starter business line, but couldn't restructure everything yet. Your file is building — reapply in a month for better terms.

actions.label: Set Up Velocity Banking
actions.description: Run your household through a line of credit instead of a checking account: park every dollar of income against the line, pay expenses from it, and chunk the surplus straight at your amortizing debt. Each positive-cash-flow month retires debt faster than a normal payment ever could — but it only works while you stay cash-flow positive, and the line can freeze in a downturn.
actions.lesson: Velocity banking is a cash-flow strategy, not a borrowing trick. Parking income on a simple-interest line shrinks the balance you pay interest on, so every surplus dollar knocks out MORE than a dollar of amortizing principal — you free capital years faster. The catch is discipline: it only accelerates while your cash flow is positive, and a credit-tight downturn can freeze the very line you're leaning on. Stay cash-flow positive and keep the line lightly used, and it's a wealth accelerator; lean on it with negative cash flow and it spirals.
actions.narrative_success: Velocity banking is live. From now on your surplus cash flow gets swept against your debt each month — accelerating payoff and freeing up your line as dry powder. Watch your cash flow: it's the engine. Stay positive and you'll retire debt fast; go negative and the strategy works against you.
actions.narrative_failure: The structure didn't fully click into place this month — your banker wants the line seasoned a little longer. Keep deposits flowing and reapply.

actions.label: Take Out a Term Loan
actions.description: Apply for a fixed-rate term loan — personal-sized before your LLC, larger and business-sized after — to fund growth or consolidate debt.
actions.lesson: Debt isn't the enemy — UNPRODUCTIVE debt is. Borrowing to grow a business that pays you back is how you buy freedom in years instead of decades. The 'safe' path — a salaried job — trades your time for the most heavily-taxed income there is.
actions.narrative_success: Approved for $10,000. The loan officer barely hesitated — your credit spoke for itself. Cash in account by Friday.
actions.narrative_failure: Declined. The loan officer said your debt-to-income was borderline. Try paying down some debt first.

actions.label: Open / Expand Business Credit Line
actions.description: Open or stack a revolving credit line in the business name — cash on demand, lower utilization, and dry powder ready when you need it.
actions.lesson: Business credit borrows on the company's name and assets — not yours. This is how you scale with other people's money without putting your household on the line. Each added line lowers utilization and sits ready as dry powder — access is an asset even when idle.
actions.narrative_success: Approved for $15,000. The banker shook your hand and said 'come back when you need more.' You will.
actions.narrative_failure: Declined — the bank pulled your personal credit and didn't like what it saw. The hard inquiry dinged your score a couple points. Pay down utilization and reapply.

actions.label: Build Banking Relationship
actions.description: Open a business account, run your deposits through their bank, and meet your banker regularly — so you're a known, trusted customer before you ever need a loan. Banks lend bigger lines, and approve far more readily, to people who already bank with them.
actions.lesson: Capital is a relationship business. Bankers fund people they know and trust — open the account, keep deposits flowing, and build the relationship BEFORE you need money. When you do apply, you'll qualify more easily and for larger amounts than a stranger walking in cold.
actions.narrative_success: The branch manager knows your name now. When you need capital, you won't be a stranger walking in cold.
actions.narrative_failure: Good meeting, but the banker transferred branches. You're starting the relationship over with someone new.

actions.label: Business Insurance Stack
actions.description: One stack of business-paid cover: income protection, critical-illness, key-person, and liability.
actions.lesson: Protect the engine before you build on it: cover your income if you can't work, get tax-free cash if you're seriously ill, replace yourself for the company, and absorb lawsuits — all tax-deductible.
actions.narrative_success: The stack is in force, all on the business books. Income protection covers your family if you can't work, critical & chronic illness riders pay you a tax-free lump sum if your health gives out, the key-person payout would fund your replacement, and the liability policy stands between a lawsuit and everything you've built. Premiums run about $550/mo.
actions.narrative_failure: Applications submitted; underwriting wants more medical records on one. The other two are active. Should fully clear next month.

actions.label: Elect S-Corp & Operating Agreement
actions.description: Put yourself on payroll, adopt an operating agreement, and elect S-Corp taxation to legally cut your self-employment tax.
actions.lesson: How your income is STRUCTURED matters as much as how much you earn. A documented salary plus S-Corp election splits salary from distributions and legally cuts self-employment tax — and the operating agreement hardens your liability shield. Levers a W-2 employee never gets.
actions.narrative_success: You're on payroll, the operating agreement is signed, and the S-Corp election is filed. Your tax advisor estimates $8,000–$12,000 in annual savings — and lenders now see a real, bankable business.
actions.narrative_failure: Payroll is running and the election is filed, but at a lower salary than planned. Savings are real, just smaller until revenue catches up.

actions.label: Reincorporate as a C-Corp
actions.description: Convert to a C-Corp — built for scale and investors. Adds a layer of corporate tax but unlocks stock options and equity grants for your team.
actions.lesson: A C-Corp trades a bit of tax efficiency for the ability to issue equity — option pools, 409A valuations, QSBS. It's the structure venture-backed companies use because ownership is the best recruiting and retention tool there is. The right move once people, not cash, are your scarcest resource.
actions.narrative_success: The C-Corp conversion is filed, the option pool is authorized, and your cap table is investor-ready. You can now put real ownership in your people's hands.
actions.narrative_failure: The conversion went through, though the option pool paperwork is still being finalized. The structure is in place to grant equity soon.

actions.label: Asset Protection Stack
actions.description: Layer a holding company, trust, and umbrella policy over your business — lawsuits hit the operating company, your wealth sits untouchable above it.
actions.lesson: A holding company owns the assets; the operating company takes the risk. Add a trust and umbrella and one lawsuit can't erase a decade of building. The wealthy protect first, then grow.
actions.narrative_success: A holding company now owns the operating business, and the attorney delivered the trust documents. Your assets are held by entities that don't have your name on the door. Bulletproof.
actions.narrative_failure: The holding structure is filed and a basic shield is in place, but the full trust setup needs more time. Partially protected.

actions.label: Form a Wyoming Holding LLC
actions.description: Stand up a Wyoming holding LLC above your business — registered agent, business address, and a real operating agreement. A clean parent entity that owns the valuable pieces, shields them from operating liabilities, and makes banks take you seriously.
actions.lesson: A holding company owns; the operating company does the risky work — so a lawsuit or bad debt against operations can't reach what's held above it. Wyoming adds strong charging-order protection and privacy. Critically, the clean setup gets your business the CORRECT NAICS industry code — without it, lenders can flag a mismatched code and decline you until it's fixed. A proper operating agreement, right NAICS code, and registered agent are exactly what underwriters want before extending real credit. Structure first, then leverage.
actions.narrative_success: The Wyoming holding LLC is filed — registered agent in Cheyenne, operating agreement executed, NAICS code assigned. It sits above your operating company, owning the valuable pieces, and your banker noticed the clean structure: your borrowing line just got friendlier.
actions.narrative_failure: The entity is formed and the registered agent is in place, but the operating agreement and asset transfers are still being finalized. The shell is up; the protection firms up as you complete the paperwork.

actions.label: Insure Your Key Operators
actions.description: Put key-man and loan-protection policies on the people running your income properties. If one is lost, the policy retires that property's specific mortgage — so a tragedy can't cascade into a margin call. Re-take it as you add assets to keep coverage current.
actions.lesson: Leverage on people and assets is only safe when the people are insured. A key-man / loan-protection policy means a sick or lost operator retires the loan on THEIR asset instead of blowing up the whole portfolio. It covers that specific debt — never a windfall — and it's what lets the wealthy borrow at scale against income-producing assets without the leverage becoming a time bomb.
actions.narrative_success: Key-man and loan-protection policies are bound on your operators.
actions.narrative_failure: Key-man and loan-protection policies are bound on your operators.

actions.label: Hire General Counsel
actions.description: Put a lawyer on retainer to review contracts, lock down IP, and handle disputes — turning ruinous lawsuits into routine correspondence.
actions.lesson: Most legal disasters are cheap to prevent and ruinous to fix. In-house counsel reviewing your contracts and IP turns existential lawsuits into routine correspondence — protection that pays for itself the first time it's needed.
actions.narrative_success: Your counsel rewrote your contracts, filed your trademarks, and put a demand-letter process in place. The next time someone threatens to 'get their lawyer involved,' yours picks up the phone.
actions.narrative_failure: Counsel is on retainer and reviewing the backlog — contracts first, IP next. The shield is going up, just not all at once.

actions.label: Advanced Tax Strategy
actions.description: Engage a year-round tax strategist — maxing retirement vehicles, cost segregation, and deductions to legally slash your tax bill.
actions.lesson: Taxes are most people's single largest lifetime expense. Year-round planning — not an April scramble — legally minimizes them. That's the difference between building wealth and funding someone else's budget.
actions.narrative_success: Your effective tax rate dropped from 32% to 21%. Retirement vehicles maxed, cost segregation done, deductions captured — all legal. Your CPA high-fived you.
actions.narrative_failure: Some strategies implemented, others need documentation. Partial savings this year, full effect next.

actions.label: Set Up Monthly Tax Reserve
actions.description: Automatically set aside a slice of revenue each month for taxes, so tax season stops being a year-end surprise.
actions.lesson: Set aside taxes every month and tax season stops being a crisis. That money was never yours — treat it that way and you'll never get blindsided.
actions.narrative_success: Tax reserve is active. Every month, a slice of revenue goes into a separate pot. Future you will be grateful.
actions.narrative_failure: Set up started but your bookkeeping needs cleanup first. Should be running next month.

actions.label: Fund Accumulation Policy
actions.description: Open an IUL policy that compounds ~7% tax-free. The cost shown is setup only — ongoing funding comes automatically from your monthly cash flow.
actions.lesson: This is your tax-advantaged money engine. Build the income and credit capacity FIRST, then open and fund it: a steady monthly contribution (minus a small ~2% cost of insurance) compounds tax-free, and you can borrow against up to 90% of the cash value anytime — no bank approval. Consistency beats lump sums.
actions.narrative_success: Policy in force and monthly funding is on. Your first $3,000 is in the cash value, growing ~7% tax-free, and automatic contributions keep feeding it. You can borrow against up to 90% of it anytime — no bank approval needed.
actions.narrative_failure: Underwriting needs more info. Should be approved next month.

actions.label: Activate Tax-Free Passive Income
actions.description: Turn on a monthly tax-free income stream drawn against your policy's cash value. Starts on the SAFE wash loan — net ~0% cost, lapse-proof — so the engine can run forever.
actions.lesson: The crown jewel. You draw tax-free income as a loan against your policy while the cash value keeps compounding — the loan is repaid from the death benefit, not your pocket. It starts on a WASH loan: the borrowed slice is parked at a matching rate, so the loan can never outrun your cash value and the policy can't lapse. Income without work, without tax, without ever selling the asset. (Want more? You can switch to a variable loan to chase the index arbitrage — but that adds real lapse risk.)
actions.narrative_success: The income switch is on. Each month the insurer wires you a tax-free check drawn against your cash value — on a safe wash loan, so it keeps compounding and can't lapse. You're being paid to own the asset.

actions.label: Fund Pre-Tax via a Defined-Benefit Plan
actions.description: Sponsor a qualified defined-benefit / cash-balance plan through your corporation and fund your policy with DEDUCTIBLE pre-tax business dollars instead of personal after-tax cash. A big tax break now and far larger contributions — but it's Traditional, not Roth.
actions.lesson: Pre-tax funding isn't a retail product — it needs a business that can sponsor a qualified plan: an S-corp or C-corp paying you W-2 wages, profitable enough to fund the defined benefit. The corporation DEDUCTS the contribution (real tax savings now, and you can shelter far more income). The trade: no basis, so the income you draw is taxable and a lapse taxes the WHOLE balance — Traditional (deduct now, taxed later) vs. the Roth-style after-tax policy (pay now, tax-free forever). Your call, and it depends on your goal: cash and shelter now, or tax-free freedom later.
actions.narrative_success: Your defined-benefit plan is set up. The corporation now funds your policy with deductible pre-tax dollars — a serious deduction every year, and far bigger contributions than after-tax would allow. Just remember: this is Traditional money. The income is taxable when you draw it, and if the policy ever lapses the whole balance is taxed.

actions.label: Switch to a Variable Policy Loan
actions.description: Move your policy loan from the safe wash (net ~0%) to a VARIABLE loan: your full cash value keeps earning the index (0–15%) while you pay a market rate (~5%). Bigger income and positive arbitrage in good years — real lapse risk in bad ones.
actions.lesson: This is the high-stakes version of the crown jewel. On a variable loan your ENTIRE cash value keeps earning the index, so in good years the ~8% growth out-earns the ~5% loan — free money on borrowed money (positive arbitrage). But the loan rate is variable and the index floors at 0% in a downturn: borrow too aggressively and a bad market lets the loan compound PAST your cash value, the policy lapses, and the gains hit you as taxable ordinary income on money you already spent. Only do this with a cushion and never max the loan. Knowing WHEN not to reach is the skill.
actions.narrative_success: You've switched to a variable policy loan. Your full cash value now rides the index — in a good market you're earning more than the loan costs, on borrowed money. But the floor is 0% and the rate floats: keep a cushion, because if a downturn lets the loan pass your cash value, the policy lapses and the taxman is waiting.

actions.label: Take a Policy Loan
actions.description: Borrow up to 90% against your policy's cash value — no credit check, no bank approval — while the cash value keeps growing.
actions.lesson: You just accessed cash tax-free — no bank, no credit check — and your money kept compounding as if you never touched it. This is how the wealthy spend without selling assets or triggering a tax bill. You became your own bank.
actions.narrative_success: Policy loan approved instantly — no credit check, no application. The money is in your account tomorrow, and your cash value keeps compounding as if you never touched it.

actions.label: Premium Financing Strategy
actions.description: Use a low-interest bank loan to fund a big policy that compounds ~7% tax-free — pocketing the spread on the bank's money. For high-net-worth players.
actions.lesson: Premium financing is leverage for the ultra-wealthy: the bank lends against your collateral to fund a tax-free asset that grows faster than the loan costs. You pocket the spread without spending your own cash — but it only works at real scale (millions in net worth) where the policy and collateral are large enough.
actions.narrative_success: The premium financing deal is structured — the bank's money, compounding tax-free in your policy.
actions.narrative_failure: The bank's terms came back tighter than expected. Try again once your collateral is stronger.

actions.label: Buy Income-Producing Property
actions.description: Buy a rental or small commercial property with the bank's money — monthly cashflow, appreciation, and tax benefits while the tenant pays it down.
actions.lesson: This is leverage. You control a six-figure income-producing asset with a fraction of your own cash — the bank funds the rest, the tenant pays it down, and it appreciates while you sleep. Paying all cash would be the INEFFICIENT move.
actions.narrative_success: Keys in hand. The duplex cash-flows $700/month after mortgage, taxes, and insurance. Passive income is real now.
actions.narrative_failure: The property closed, but the inspection found issues. Repairs ate into your projections. It'll cash-flow, just not as much.

actions.label: Become a Private Lender
actions.description: Become the bank: lend your capital to other entrepreneurs' deals, earning 8-15% interest secured by their assets.
actions.lesson: Now YOU'RE the bank. Your capital earns double-digit returns secured by someone else's assets — money working hard while you don't lift a finger. That's passive income.
actions.narrative_success: First deal funded — $20,000 at 12%, secured by the borrower's equipment. Monthly checks arriving. You're the bank now.
actions.narrative_failure: The deal funded, but the borrower's been slow on payments. Secured, so you're protected — but it's stressful.

actions.label: Acquire a Competitor
actions.description: Buy a smaller competitor — its cash flow becomes your passive income, and the deal's write-offs cut your taxes.
actions.lesson: At scale you buy income, not just customers. A well-structured acquisition pays you twice: passive cash flow from day one, plus depreciation and amortization write-offs that legally shrink your tax bill. The deal funds itself.

actions.label: Fast Working Capital
actions.description: Need cash fast? $25,000 by tomorrow, approved on your sales alone — no credit check, no collateral. They just take a small cut of your revenue until it's paid back.
actions.lesson: That was a merchant cash advance: $25k now, but you repay ~$32,500 — a 30% fee — and they siphon 20% of your revenue every month until it's cleared, choking cash flow for months. No credit check (they pre-approve on your sales), so it spares your score but bleeds your top line. Real capital comes from credit and banking you build BEFORE you're desperate.
actions.narrative_success: Approved on your revenue alone — $25,000 hits your account overnight, no credit pull. The catch you skimmed: they now take 20% of every dollar you bring in until they've collected about $32,500 — a 30% fee on top.

actions.label: Off-Market 'Guaranteed' Fund
actions.description: An invite-only placement a peer swears by — guaranteed double-digit returns, insider access, closing this week. Put your capital to work in something the public never sees.
actions.lesson: Guaranteed + exclusive + closing-this-week is the fingerprint of a scam — often reaching you through someone you trust. Real returns are never guaranteed, and the rush is the tell. You lost most of your capital plus audit and legal exposure. The crown jewel is structured, vetted passive income — not yield chased into the dark.
actions.narrative_success: You wired the funds before the 'window closed.' The monthly statements looked great — until they stopped coming. The manager is unreachable, the fund's 'assets' can't be verified, and now there are lawyers and the IRS asking questions.
